title: BYU Civil Engineering Program Assessment Manual
text:
Introduction
This document describes the processes and methods we use to assess and evaluate the BYU Civil Engineering (BS) program. ABET criterion 4 states:
This manual documents these processes and provides a record of historical assessment data. The program uses five categories of assessments:

title: BYU Civil Engineering Program Assessment Manual
text:
Evaluation and Continuous Improvement Process
Each summer, the CE undergraduate committee evaluates recent assessment data. The evaluation process results in a determination of the program’s strengths and weaknesses. The process also recommends actions to resolve the identified weaknesses. Potential actions may include changes to the following:

program courses, requirements, or prerequisites
course content or learning activities
course assessments
performance indicators or associated rubrics

The undergraduate committee documents the assessment evidence, evaluated strengths and weaknesses, and proposed actions in the program stewardship report. This report is reviewed by the department faculty and approved in a meeting before August 31 of the year of the report.
The undergraduate committee takes responsibility to implement the recommended actions. Changes to program courses, requirements, or prerequisites are approved by faculty vote and submitted to the university curriculum committee and registrar for approval. When recommending changes to courses or course activities, the program committee works closely with the course instructors to design and implement the proposed actions.
Individual instructors make improvements to course activities, pedagogy, and content based on data obtained through direct experience and student evaluation comments. These are important data that may lead to recommended course and program changes, but are not a formal assessment tool.

title: 1  Student Outcomes
text:
1.1 Complex Problems
The program assesses each of its seven student outcomes using performance indicators and associated rubrics.
Each academic year, we assess and evaluate student learning outcomes based on the schedule in Table 1.1. This rolling three-year schedule allows us to assess each student outcome twice during a six-year ABET evaluation cycle.
We attempt to assess each student outcome at two levels:
A matrix mapping each student outcome to the courses is shown in Table 1.2.

title: 1  Student Outcomes
text:
An ability to identify, formulate, and solve complex engineering problems by applying principles of engineering, science, and mathematics.​







WarningABET Definition



The ABET definition of “Complex Problems” is

Complex engineering problems include one or more of the following characteristics: involving wide-ranging or conflicting technical issues, having no obvious solution, addressing problems not encompassed by current standards and codes, including many component parts or sub-problems, involving multiple disciplines, or having significant consequences in a range of contexts.

It is understood that just because a problem is “hard” does not mean it is complex. Similarly, a problem may have many sub-steps, but if they are not integrated or coordinated, that does not make it complex.



Performance Indicators
We evaluate this outcome based on three performance indicators. Students should:

Identify the problem parameters and given values.
Formulate a solution strategy.
Evaluate the best alternative from many possible solutions.



Rubric










Indicator
Unsatisfactory
Developing
Meets Expectations
Exceeds Expectations




1. Identify
Fails to identify given parameters.
Identifies some given parameters.
Identifies all provided parameters.
Identifies all parameters including unreasonable parameters or those requiring assumptions.


2. Formulate
Fails to formulate a solution strategy.
Formulates a strategy with limitations.
Formulates a solution strategy based in accepted methods.
Formulates a strategy that adjusts for the weaknesses of existing techniques.


3. Evaluate
Fails to evaluate alternative solutions.
Evaluates an alternative solution.
Evaluates alternative solutions.
Evaluates alternatives with a consideration of human needs.





Assessment
We assess this student outcome in the following courses with the listed assessments:
Formative

CCE 103: Engineering Mechanics – Statics

Identify: Final exam question
Formulate: Final exam question

CCE 203: Engineering Mechanics – Materials

Identify: Final exam question
Formulate: Final exam question


Summative

CE 321: Structural Analysis

Identify: Truss analysis project
Formulate: Truss analysis project
Evaluate: Truss analysis project

CE 331: Hydrology

Identify: Term project
Formulate: Term project
Evaluate: Term project

CE 341: Soil Mechanics

Identify: Final exam question
Formulate: Final exam question
Evaluate: Final exam question

CE 351: Environmental Engineering

Identify: Final exam question
Formulate: Final exam question
Evaluate: Final exam question

CE 361: Transportation Engineering

Identify: Pavement management homework
Formulate: Pavement management homework
Evaluate: Pavement management homework


We will assess this student outcome in the 2026-27 academic year.

title: 1  Student Outcomes
text:
1.2 SO 2 Design
An ability to apply engineering design to produce solutions that meet specified needs with consideration of public health, safety, and welfare as well as global, cultural, social, environmental, and economic factors.

Performance Indicators
We evaluate this outcome based on four performance indicators. Students should:

Develop the criteria against which the design will be judged. They may include safety requirements, performance expectations, or industry standards.
Select appropriate computational, experimental, or qualitative methods to develop the design, taking it from problem to solution.
Justify the selection of a preferred solution from among multiple alternatives.
Consider public health, safety, and welfare as well as global, cultural, social, environmental, and economic factors.











Indicator
Unsatisfactory
Developing
Meets Expectations
Exceeds Expectations




1. Develop design criteria
Little recognition of criteria or selection of irrelevant criteria
Partial recognition of relevant criteria
Thorough recognition of relevant criteria
Thorough recognition of relevant criteria, plus optional ones


2. Select methods
Poor matching of methods to problem
Partial selection or justification of relevant methods
Selection and justification of methods relevant to solving problem
Selection and justification of relevant methods, acknowledging limitations


3. Recommend design
No design, wrong design, or unjustified design
Partially acceptable design and justification
Acceptable design justified by methods and criteria
Acceptable, justified design chosen from multiple alternatives


4. Consider factors

title: 1  Student Outcomes
text:
1.4 SO 4 Ethics
An ability to recognize ethical and professional responsibilities in engineering situations and make informed judgments, which must consider the impact of engineering solutions in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.

Performance Indicators
We evaluate this outcome based on three performance indicators. Students should:

Recognize personal and professional responsibilities towards others including employees, clients, the wider profession, and the public.
Recognize moral and ethical dilemmas in engineering situations and make judgments to identify appropriate resolutions.
Consider the impacts of an engineering solution in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.



Rubric










Indicator
Unsatisfactory
Developing
Meets Expectations
Exceeds expectations




Recognize responsibilities
Does not recognize or identify professional responsibilities.
Recognizes some professional responsibilities, but misses at least one.
Recognizes professional responsibilities towards all key stakeholders
Demonstrates interest in and motivation to serve society through the profession.


Make judgments
Does not identify ethical dilemmas, and or develops inappropriate resolutions
Misidentifies ethical dilemmas, and or develops inadequate resolutions.
Properly identifies ethical dilemmas and identifies appropriate resolutions.
Actively considers ethical challenges that may arise in professional contexts and seeks to mitigate them.


Consider impacts
Fails to identify impacts in any contexts.
Identifies impacts in some contexts, but fails to identify likely impacts in others.
Identifies likely impacts in a variety of contexts.
Actively seeks to consider the ethical impact of engineering situations in global, economic, environmental, and societal contexts.





Assessment
We assess this learning outcome in three courses:

CCE 102: Sustainable Infrastructure (Introductory)
CE 291 R: Civil Engineering Seminar (Reinforcing)
CE 472: Civil Engineering Capstone (Evaluation)

We evaluated this outcome most recently in Winter 2025. All graduating students take CE 472 in Winter semester. Students take CE 291 R each semester from their sophomore year onward. CCE 102 may be taken in Fall or Winter semesters, so this is approximately a 50% sample of the students in the program. CCE 102 also includes students who are in the CFM and the FM programs.
Figure 1.2 shows the share of students at each performance level for each indicator for each course, alongside the threshold of 75%. By taking CE 472, 75% of students meet the expectations for each indicator.








Figure 1.2: Winter 2025 Assessment of Student Outcome 4

title: 1  Student Outcomes
text:
1.5 SO 5 Teamwork
An ability to function effectively on a team whose members together provide leadership, create a collaborative and inclusive environment, establish goals, plan tasks, and meet objectives.

Performance Indicators
We evaluate this outcome based on three performance indicators. Students should: 1. Show leadership through accountability and service 2. Contribute to a collaborative and inclusive environment where all team members feel a sense of belonging. 3. Meet team objectives through establishing goals and planning tasks.
We assess student performance on these indicators using the following rubric.


Assessment
  | Indicator | Unsatisfactory | Developing | Meets Expectations | Exceeds expectations | |———————————|———————————————————–|—————————————————————————————|—————————————————————————————-|————————————————————————————————–| | 1. Accountability and service | Does not accomplish assigned tasks | Accomplishes assigned tasks but is delayed or requires reminders | Accomplishes tasks on time | Accomplishes tasks on time, and helps team members accomplish their roles | | 2. Contribute to team belonging | No or negative involvement in team activities. | Involved in team activities and makes some effort to engage others. | Actively involved in team activities and encourages engagement from others | Drives team activities and motivates productive engagement from others | | 3. Meet team objectives | Fails to identify reasonable goals or appropriate tasks. | Develops goals and tasks that are infeasible, or that required substantial revision. | Develops goals and a task plan that results in meeting the assignment expectations. | Develops goals and a resilient task plan that results in exceeding the assignment expectations. |








Figure 1.3: Fall 2025 and Winter 2026 Assessment of Student Outcome 5

title: 1  Student Outcomes
text:
1.6 SO 6 Experiments and Data
An ability to develop and conduct appropriate experimentation, analyze and interpret data, and use engineering judgment to draw conclusions

Performance Indicators
We evaluate this outcome based on four performance indicators. Students should:

Identify conditions: Identify what conditions need to be controlled or varied in an experiment in order to obtain data needed to test a hypothesis.
Select methods: Select methods, materials, and/or equipment that will generate the desired data.
Conduct: Conduct the experiment and keep appropriate records.
Analyze and conclude: Analyze data using scientific techniques and draw supported conclusions.



Rubric










Indicator
Unsatisfactory
Developing
Meets Expectations
Exceeds expectations




Identify conditions
Does not distinguish controls and variables; attempts multiple variables in one experiment
Identifies some controls and variables
Correctly identifies all controls and variables
Correctly identifies all controls and variables as well as possible confounding conditions


Select methods
Does not match methods to experiment
Selects some adequate methods, materials, or equipment
Selects adequate methods, materials, and equipment; can justify choice
Selects adequate methods, materials, and equipment; can justify choice; may use standards


Conduct
Careless setup or execution of experiment; little or no recordkeeping; inattentive to experiment
Some problems in setup or execution; keeps some records
Carefully sets up and executes experiment; records include photos, notes, observations, and potential errors
Carefully sets up and executes experiment; records include photos, notes, observations, and potential errors or limitations


Analyze and conclude
Little or no analysis of data; incorrect analysis of data; no conclusion; concludes without data; concludes contradictory to data
Shows some analysis by figures, equations, statistics, etc., though perhaps with mistakes; may conclude without data or contradictory to data
Demonstrates thorough analysis by figures, equations, statistics, etc.; concludes based on data and in accordance with data; recognizes assumptions
Demonstrates thorough analysis by figures, equations, statistics, etc.; concludes based on data and in accordance with data; recognizes assumptions; acknowledges civil engineering applications of results





Assessment
We assess this learning outcome at three levels

CCE 270: Computer Methods (Introductory)
CE 232: Fluid Mechanics (Reinforcing)
CE Breadth classes (Evaluation):

CCE 306: Civil Engineering Materials
CE 341: Soil Mechanics
CE 351: Environmental Engineering


All CE students must take one of the three breadth classes where we assess this outcome, though many will take more than one. We evaluated this outcome most recently in Winter 2025.

title: 2  Exit Surveys and Interviews
text:
2.1 Exit Surveys
Each December and April, the program sends a survey to all of our graduating students. The purpose of this survey is to collect data on the following items:

title: 2  Exit Surveys and Interviews
text:
Student contact information for alumni tracking

2.1.1 Survey Questions


2.1.2 Results

title: 2  Exit Surveys and Interviews
text:
2.2 Exit Interviews
Each April, the undergraduate committee samples graduating students and interviews them in personal conversation. The purpose of this interview is to qualitatively assess student-observed program strengths and weakness.
The CE undergraduate coordinator assigns three to four graduating seniors to each member of the committee. The committee members arrange to speak privately with each student for fifteen to twenty minutes. The student should be invited to direct the discussion, but possible questions include:

What are the most positive learning experiences you have had?
Have you had any less successful learning experiences?

Each committee member makes notes of the student’s responses. The assessmentcoordinator consolidates these notes into a coherent statment and posts it in the space below.

Class of 2026Class of 2025





Students described the civil engineering program as offering strong overall preparation, with a solid breadth of coursework and particularly effective introductory classes that sparked interest in the major. Many felt well prepared for the FE exam and appreciated practical, real-world applications in courses like CE 471, as well as hands-on experiences through competition teams, research, study abroad, and TA roles. Faculty were consistently viewed as enthusiastic, accessible, and invested in student success, especially in smaller upper-division classes where interaction was more direct. The program fostered a strong sense of belonging through clubs, study groups, and shared spaces, supported by active (though sometimes inconsistent) faculty involvement. Students valued flexible curriculum elements like the junior core options, and many appreciated opportunities to integrate faith meaningfully into their education. Physical spaces such as labs and common areas also contributed positively to collaboration and community.
At the same time, students identified several persistent challenges. Seminar was widely seen as repetitive and inefficient, with too many guest speakers and not enough practical skill development, and its scheduling sometimes conflicted with internships. There were concerns about uneven course quality, disruptions from instructor turnover, limited TA support in upper-division courses, and heavy workloads that restricted extracurricular involvement. Technical skills like CAD, Revit, and Excel were not consistently reinforced, leading to skill loss over time. Writing requirements—particularly ENGL 316—were viewed as overly burdensome and less relevant than discipline-specific alternatives like CE 471, with some students suggesting earlier placement in the curriculum. Capstone grading was sometimes perceived as overly nitpicky, and some assignments felt like busywork. Students also noted scheduling limitations (e.g., course conflicts, weaker winter offerings), a lack of advanced or graduate-level electives, and a need for clearer guidance on electives and career pathways. Additional suggestions included improving inclusivity and representation (especially for women), expanding interdisciplinary and coding opportunities, increasing faculty engagement in student organizations, and addressing administrative friction points such as curriculum changes and the graduate application process.

title: 3  Fundamentals of Engineering
text:
The Fundamentals of Engineering (FE) examination provides important external evidence of our program’s effectiveness in training competent civil engineering professionals. University policies prohibit us from requiring students to take the exam before graduation, but we strongly recommend that our students do so and offer an FE preparation course to help them.
The share of students passing the FE exam is shown in Figure 3.1. BYU students pass the exam at a rate considerably higher than other ABET-accredited civil engineering programs. This data is for students currently enrolled in the program, or graduating in the 12 months prior to taking the exam. We do not include alumni who take the FE more than one year after graduation in this assessment.








Figure 3.1: FE Exam pass rate by semester, BYU Civil and ABET comparator group.





We also use the FE exam to assess discipline and topic subscores. Figure 3.2 provides an interactive graphic where you can choose an FE exam category and compare BYU civil engineering students and recent alumni against an “ABET comparator” group of similar institutions. BYU students generally exceed the ABET comparator group mean in all categories for almost all periods, but fall within a single standard deviation of that mean. There are a few exceptions to this performance, with BYU students modestly below the ABET comparator mean in some categories for some periods. These include the “Ethics and Professional Practice” and the “Construction Engineering” categories.









Figure 3.2: FE Exam category subscores from Fall 2022 onwards. Interactive application on ShinyApps.









NoteAssessment Practices





NCEES makes aggregated FE scores available to colleges for assessment purposes. The College of Engineering at BYU has a login name to this system, which is shared with the department academic advisor.
Each August and each February, the assessment coordinator retrieves the most recent scores from the advisor and adds them to data records in this repository. There are two places where these data are stored:

The data/fe.xlsx spreadsheet contains the data from the advisor’s login as well as a worksheet of the general exam pass rates.
The apps/data.csv files contains the exam subscores in machine-readable format.

To update the report, the assessment coordinator does the following:

Runs the chunks on this page to update the FE pass rate figure.
Generates the shiny app in the apps folder with the new data.
Uploads the shiny app to the posit connect cloud and updates the links to the app in the report.
Edits the report text to reflect any additional evaluation of the assessment data.

title: 4  Student Course Evaluations
text:
In completing the evaluation of each course, students indicate how well they felt the course advanced the Aims of a BYU Education, which are that a BYU Education should be

Spiritually strenghening
Intellectually enlarging
Character building
Leading to lifelong learning and service

Students rate each course on a five-point scale. Figure 4.1 shows the Department averages for each course over the last few years. These evaluations have remained largely stable across all categories, but these numbers unfortunately cannot be disaggregated by program.






Figure 4.1: Department course evaluation averages.









NoteAssessment Practices





The program coordinator has access to a dashboard at data.byu.edu that presents the aggregated course evaluation data.
Each May, the assessment coordinator retrieves the most recent course evaluation data from the portal and updates the image in the data/student_ratings.png file and renders the page.



The department also uses student evaluation data to identify strengths and weaknesses in faculty development. This information is not used in program assessment.

title: 5  Student Progress
text:
5.1 Time to graduation
The University tracks time to graduation for each student in number of enrollments, or the number of semesters and terms that a student enrolls in courses. This controls for mission deferments but not for transfers. The relevant statistic is the share of students who graduate within twelve enrollments.
Figure 5.1 shows the share of CE program students who graduate within the specified numer of enrollments. This includes all students who entered the university in the 2016-17 through 2020-21 school years. This time period is defined as five years of entering students, four complete academic years ago.
For this period, 274 of 448 total students graduated within twelve enrollments, for a share of 61 percent. The time to graduation for each student is not given to us by the university. But we can track the aggregate statistic over time. Figure 5.2 shows the share of CE program students who graduate within twelve enrollments for five year aggregated cohorts. This number has been dropping for as long as data are available.
Figure 5.2: Percent graduated within twelve enrollments, five year aggregated cohorts in year ending.

title: 5  Student Progress
text:
Figure 5.1: Enrollments to graduation for CE program students. Cohorts entering from 2016-17 through 2020-21 school years.











Warning



It should be noted that if a student entered the university in 2020-21 and only enrolls in fall and winter semesters, they would have only completed ten enrollments by the end of the 2024-25 academicyear.








NoteAssessment Practices





The program coordinator has access to a dashboard at data.byu.edu that presents the time to graduation data. The dashboard is located at the above website under Student Success Indicators > Enrollments to Graduation. The resulting dashboard allows the user to filter to specific programs and other student types.
New data should be added to this report each June. The program assessment coordinator should obtain the new data and update this page.
The image showing the cohort progression should be for five years of students, ending four academic years ago. The image should be saved in the data/graduation folder and the link updated in this report. The numeric information beneath this image should be added to the table in data/graduation/cohorts_in_12.xlsx, and the page should be rendered.

title: 5  Student Progress
text:
5.2 DEW Rates
The university has asked us to track courses and students that earn a D, E, or W grade. We are developing tools to track this information.
